entry.title: Offer Approval in SmartRecruiters: Overview, Features, and Configuration Guide
entry.content:
Recruitment processes in modern organizations require multiple levels of validation before an offer is extended to a candidate. Approval workflows ensure compliance with company policies, budget approvals, and hiring governance.

The SmartRecruiters platform provides a flexible approval framework that allows organizations to configure approval chains for both job requisitions and job offers, ensuring transparency and control throughout the hiring lifecycle.

This article provides a detailed overview of Offer Approval in SmartRecruiters, including configuration steps, workflow design, nd best practices.

Overview of Offer Approval in SmartRecruiters

Offer approvals ensure that offers are reviewed internally before they are sent to candidates.
Offer approval workflows are typically triggered after the candidate reaches the Offer stage in the hiring process.

  • Key Features of Offer Approvals

Key capabilities of SmartRecruiters offer management include:

  • Offer letter templates
  • Offer clause libraries
  • Automated approval routing
  • Real-time approval notifications
  • Digital offer acceptance

Offer approvals can be configured based on job roles, departments, or compensation structures.

  • Types of Offer Approval Chains

SmartRecruiters provides multiple types of approval chains depending on business needs.

  1. Sequential Approval Chain: Approvals occur in a specific order.
  1. Parallel Approval Chain: All approvers receive the request simultaneously.
  1. Per-Job Approval Chain: Recruiters can select approved manually while creating the job.
  1. Integration Approval Chain: Approvals are managed through external integrations or APIs.

Configuration steps of Offer Approval

Below are the steps to configure offer workflows.

  1. Navigate to: Settings → Configuration → Offer Approvals

  1. Navigate to: Offer approvals chains → Click New Chain

  1. Enter the Offer approval chain details and select a field from the approval fields on which approval will trigger.

  1. Enable the " Pre-set an approval chain button if you want to define an approval workflow. Otherwise, if no approval chain is specified here, users will be able to choose approvers before sending for approval

  1. Select Approval Type

  1. Select Approvers from the drop-down, click on Add to chain, and click on the save button.

Offer Approval Settings

SmartRecruiters provides additional configuration options to enhance the approval process.

  1. Approver access to the offer
    Remove approvers access to the offer after the offer has been approved
  2. Delegate Approvals
    Users can assign another user to approve offers during their absence.
  3. Authentication Requirement
    Offer approvals can be accessed without logging into SmartRecruiters.

Conclusion
Offer approvals are critical components of a structured recruitment process. With configurable approval chains, flexible workflows, and automated notifications, SmartRecruiters enables organizations to maintain hiring governance while ensuring a smooth candidate experience.

By implementing well-designed approval workflows, companies can accelerate hiring decisions, maintain compliance, and deliver professional offers to top talent.

entry.title: Dos and Don’ts: Key User Custom Fields in Add-Ons Based on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Have you ever been in a situation where you developed an SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Scalable Delivery add-on and you wanted to add extension fields extending SAP standard objects to your add-on? Are you wondering how to do that and what to consider?
If your answer is yes, then this blog post is just for you. In this blog, we explain which options you have and what to consider in this situation.

Key User Custom Fields vs Developer Extensibility Custom Fields

When adding extension fields to an SAP standard object in your scalable delivery add-on (from now on, we’ll use add-on as abbreviation for scalable delivery add-on) you can choose to use:

  1. key user custom fields or
  2. developer extensibility custom fields.

The pros and cons for and against the two options are discussed in this blog:
Custom Fields in S/4HANA: Key User versus Developer Extensibility
Please note that beyond the pros and cons listed in this blog, you may choose “developer extensibility only”, for example, to keep the software logistics of your add-on as simple as possible.

In addition, consider that the scope of the SAP standard applications enabled for key user custom fields is much broader than the scope of those enabled for developer extensibility custom fields. If you choose “developer extensibility only”, make sure that you really only need to extend SAP standard applications which are enabled for developer extensibility custom fields.

The scope limitations of developer extensibility custom field-enabling are:

  • developer extensibility custom fields are only supported for RAP-based applications
  • developer extensibility custom fields do not support SAP GUI UIs
  • developer extensibility custom fields do not support SOAP APIs

Please note that when you create key user custom fields for an add-on, one more software component is needed that’s exclusively used for key user extensions. It’s created automatically. This happens when you create a namespace and activate key user extensibility for this namespace using the Maintain Namespaces app (details are explained below in the “preparation steps” section).  Remember to maintain key user custom fields in the Custom Fields app and in the customizing tenant, and not via ABAP Development Tools (hereafter called “ADT”) in the development tenant.

Before you can start creating key user custom fields for your add-on, there are some preparation steps needed. Let’s have a look at these preparation steps in more detail.

Preparation steps
1. First, you need a namespace. In the Landscape Portal, go to the Maintain Namespaces app. Here, you’ll see a list of namespaces you already have, or you can request a new namespace. In the latter case, you need an S-user for your company which has authorization to create namespaces.


Request namespace

In the same app, install the namespace in your development system. This is needed to make sure that all the key user custom              fields you want to include in your add-on utilize this namespace. You need to install the namespace in your test system, too, to              be able to transport your development into your test environment to validate it.

2. Next, log on to your development tenant and go to the Manage Software Components app. Within the app, you can create a new software component for your namespace which contains your developer extensibility objects. We’ll call this software component “developer extensibility software component”.


Create the developer extensibility software component

Afterwards, you need to clone this new software component within the same app. This automatically creates the following objects based on the entered name, which can be used in ADT for your Cloud development:

  1. developer extensibility software component (e.g. /S4PET/CLOUDDEV)
  2. structure package (e.g. /S4PET/CLOUDDEV)

Now, log on to your customizing tenant and go to the  Extensibility Settings app where you can see all available namespaces for key user extensibility. To create custom fields in your namespace, you must activate key user extensibility for the given namespace by selecting the “Activate Key User Extensibility” button. Enter a name for the key user software component that will be created within the namespace.

Activate key user extensibility and assign a user to this namespace

Click “Confirm”. Based on the name you’ve entered, the following objects are created for your key user development and automatically assigned to a software collection:

  1. key user extensibility software component (e.g. /S4PET/KEYUSER)
  2. key user extensibility development package (e.g. /S4PET/KEYUSER_EXT)
  3. key user extensibility software component relation (e.g. /S4PET/KEYUSER)
  4. software collection (e.g. /S4PET/KEYUSER)

In addition, you must assign your user to a namespace. If you don’t do this, your key user custom fields will still have “YY1_” as a namespace and won’t be included in the add-on.

 

3. To be able to create your own developer extensibility objects (e.g. an ABAP class) for your add-on, you first need to create a new development package in ADT in the development tenant underneath your structure package (this was automatically created during preparation step 2).  

Create developer extensibility development package

To be able to program against your key user custom fields from your developer extensibility objects, you must declare a software component relation from your developer extensibility software component to your key user extensibility software component. Just create a "Software Component Relation" object within the structure package. The object must have the same name as your developer extensibility software component where your objects will be located, and from which you want to access your key user custom fields.


Declare software component relation in your developer extensibility software component

Within this software component relation object, you maintain a dependency on your objects in the key user extensibility software component (see also Using Software Components). If you don’t do this, you will get ATC errors in all your developer extensibility objects which reference key user custom fields.

4. If you plan to use your key user custom field in a developer extensibility behavior definition (BDEF) object to specify field control for this key user custom field, you should additionally also maintain the software component relation for the key user extensibility software component (automatically created during preparation step 2). There, you must maintain access permissions for the developer extensibility software component in ADT in the development tenant.

Walking through an example step by step

Now that you’re done with the preparation work, learn how you can use key user custom fields in your add-on following a simple example:
We want to create an ABAP class which selects all Sales Documents from the SAP-delivered Sales Document CDS view. After that, we want to extend this CDS view with a new custom field and select this field.

1. In ADT (development tenant), a new ABAP class is created within the development package (see preparation step 4). This ABAP class contains a simple select on CDS view I_SalesDocument and prints out the sales document ID in the console.

Example developer extensibility class

2. Log on to your customizing tenant and open the Custom Fields app. In our example, we create a new custom field of type Checkbox for the Sales Document business context in the namespace (see preparation step 3).

Create key user custom field

As soon as the business context Sales Document gets extended by a new custom field, the CDS view I_SalesDocument gets extended by this field, too (this has been configured by SAP in that way).



Enable the custom field for CDS view I_SalesDocument 

3. In ADT (development tenant), you can now consume the key user custom field that you’ve created within the ABAP class you created earlier.


Consume the key user custom field in your developer extensibility class

4. Without a dependency declaration in the developer extensibility software relation component (as described in preparation step 4) you will either receive errors by the syntax check on your ABAP class, or by running the ATC check directly. The error message could then look like this: “CDS Entity <name of CDS extend> of software component <name of key user software component> is used without declared dependency.”


Error messages when dependency declaration in the developer extensibility software relation component is missing

To fix the errors, you need to go to the developer extensibility software component relation that you created earlier (see preparation step 4) and add a dependency to the key user software component where the custom field resides.


Dependency declaration in the developer extensibility software relation component

5. Now that you’re done with development, the next step is to release everything and test it in the test system afterwards. First, transport the custom field. To do that, log on to the customizing tenant and open the Export Software Collection app. There, search for the key user software collection created in preparation step 3 (e.g. /S4PET/KEYUSER). The key user software component relation that was created automatically and the development package are already part of the software collection. Add the key user custom field via the “Add Items” button. After that, export it into the test system. When the export is finished, go back to ADT (development tenant) and release the transport request with the developer extensibility objects (containing e.g. the developer extensibility software component relation, ABAP class).

6. The next step is to import into the test system. First, log on to the test tenant and navigate to the Import Collection app. In the list of software collections, find your key user software collection (Type: “Key User Extensibility”) and the developer software collection (Type: “Developer Extensibility”).
First, you need to import version 0 of the key user software collection (containing only the software component) by clicking “Check and Import”. Next, version 0 of the developer software collection needs to be imported. When this is done, you can continue to import the other versions (containing the objects you’ve created) of the key user software collection and the developer software collection. When the imports are finished, test your development in the test system.

7. When everything is tested successfully, the next step is to register and build (Deliver a Product) the add-on product in the landscape portal. The commit IDs that you need can be found in the “Import Collection” app in the details for the imported software collection under “Execution Messages”. For the developer extensibility software component, the commit IDs can also be found in the “Manage Software Components” app.

Now that you know how to use key user custom fields in an add-on, let’s go ahead and have a look at the things you should avoid doing.

Don’ts

Within the key user extensibility scope, only key user custom fields can be delivered as part of an add-on. Other key user extension types are not supported. As a consequence, you can't maintain features in the “Custom Fields” app that depend on other key user extension types such as custom business objects or custom business logic.

One of the issues that may occur is that you may accidentally define the software component relation incorrect or not at all. If this is the case, you’ll get ATC errors when you build your add-on product. This needs to be fixed as described above.

Another prominent issue is that the respective key user custom fields may not have been assigned to the key user software collection before exporting, or that the transport request with the developer extensibility objects that consume the custom fields has been released and imported first. This results in syntax errors in the target system. In this case, export and import the key user software collection with the respective custom fields. Then, the syntax errors will disappear.

Please note that all objects need to be active. It’s not possible to export a software collection which contains an unpublished key user custom field. Additionally, it’s not possible to release a transport request in ADT which contains inactive objects.

Do’s

Another problem that can occur is that SAP may have adjusted the content enabled for key user custom fields, and because of that, you need to republish your affected key user custom fields. Scalable delivery add-ons can be used within SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition only, and all involved (partner and customer) systems will be upgraded with each SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition release.

In general, you need to build a new version of your add-on product once for every SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition release so that your add-on is always installable for customers.

Technical changes by SAP on extended SAP objects (e.g. a migration from V1 views to V2 views) may require that custom fields in the add-on product need to be adapted. In this case, you will automatically get a so-called rework task for the impacted fields within the Custom Fields app to republish the fields. After republishing, you have to export the software collection and build a new add-on version containing the respective changes.

Rework task for the impacted fields

That’s it! Congratulations!
You successfully made your way through the Do’s and Don’ts of Key User Custom Fields @ SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition scalable add-ons.

Please note that this blog has been elaborated by a team of experts. I want to thank Yasmina van Rooij, Christian Fuhlbruegge, René Dehn, Kai Dehmann, and Mario Dietrich Collada for their extremely helpful contributions.

Feedback, Comments, or Questions:

You’re cordially invited to provide feedback, comments, or questions in the comments section below. Please follow our profiles to get updates when we publish more posts on these topics.
Feel invited to post and answer questions here (https://answers.sap.com/tags/270c4f37-c335-46e1-bfad-a256637d5e26) and read other posts on the topic (https://blogs.sap.com/tags/270c4f37-c335-46e1-bfad-a256637d5e26/).

Other related blog posts and SAP help documents:

  1. Developing an Extension Using Key User Extensibility
  2. Developing ABAP Extensions Using Scalable Delivery
  3. Software Component Relations
  4. Extensibility - General Availability of Scalable Solution for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  5. Developing an Extension Using Key User Extensibility

entry.title: Re: Claude Code via MCP: Poor man's Joule, or a practical tool?
entry.content:
Day 9, a Weekend session

(do) The maintenance

Not so much to report today on account of the large amount of maintenance tasks that I had to do. Implemented some security on the VPS where my 2023 ABAP Cloud Developer Trial lives (any news on the 2025 version?), and which was unsecured. Redirected the Docker Container ports to localhost, started the firewall, that kind of thing. Claude was helping me a lot. He also suggested moving the vibing-steampunk MCP Server from my local Mac to the VPS. Good idea, did that too. Actually spent quite a lot of time on all that, because I had to open an SSH tunnel to the VPS and make sure it started automatically, so that my SAP Gui and Eclipse worked. Then another tunnel for the Windows for ARM in my Parallels virtual machine. Created SAP Users for the MCP Servers... en fin, a lot of admin work.

Learned a lot though, which is good. For example, I was today's years old when I learned the command /context.

Yeah, still learning the basics 😅

Speaking of context, it was in the news recently that Claude Code on Max Plans now has 1 million tokens. Nice, can't wait for it to trickle down to the Pro plan.

 

(don't) Reinvent the wheel

Then it was time to start tackling the biggest obstacle in my quest to set up a system that builds Fiori apps end-to end: The fact that the Models have not able to create a functioning front-end. In order not to reinvent the wheel, I installed the Fiori MCP Server by... not sure who the author is, there are so many contributors👨‍🍳. Installed it on the VPS, like the others. Haven't tried it yet.

In order yes to reinvent the wheel, I had a discussion with Claude Chat about why oh why are the models so useless at creating freestyle Fiori apps. He used some strong words about SAPUI5 being a massive, opinionated framework with its own MVC pattern, OData model binding, Navigation patterns, Save/edit cycle, Context binding on detail pages and more. He suggested that we should inspect the Fiori app that Codex built, and based on the inspection results create a Fiori Skill. Why not, let's give it a try. The inspection yielded this:

Claude then created said Fiori Skill, which I installed on Project level. When I went to try it, I realised I had removed and restarted the A4H Docker Container during the maintenance, which means that the Northwind RAP application I had developed with Codex was gone. I need to reinstall it from the GitHub Repo.

So much for that.

 

Codex does not want to be left out

I really like my mini Agentic Engineering setup for Claude I described in my previous entry - with 2 levels of Agents and a couple of tight Skills. Wanted to replicate the same, but for Codex. So I pointed ChatGPT at my Agentic Setup GitHub Repo, and told him basically "Make the same, but for Codex". He said "Well, this isn't very good" and proceeded to create something which in my opinion is worse. You can check it out here. Created more Agents such as abap_rap_writer, data_metadata_checker and transport_scope_reviewer - no clue what those guys are supposed to do. The instructions are pretty general.

So what I have done now is I have created two local templates for these Agentic Setups - one for Claude, one for Codex.

 

New idea: Heard somewhere that people were using their Obsidian vaults for Model Context and maybe even to hold the Skills! I'd love that, and I'll definitely explore that idea.

And last but not least, spent some time on LinkedIn, where I found a bunch of articles about what you guys have been doing in this space (which is what I should have read in the first place 😅 ). I haven't been to LinkedIn in ages, didn't know it could be such a good source.

Anyways. I'll conclude this Blog with the next entry, which will be the 10th.

entry.title: How I teach Claude Code to work my way
entry.content:
The Problem With "Just Prompting"

Here's what used to happen: I'd open Claude Code, describe what I wanted, and the AI would immediately start writing code. Sometimes it was great. More often, it solved the wrong problem, or solved the right one in a way that clashed with the rest of my codebase. I'd spend more time course-correcting than I saved.

The issue wasn't the AI's coding ability. It was the lack of process. When a senior developer tackles a feature, they don't just start typing. They read the existing code, consider the architecture, think about edge cases, maybe sketch something out. I wanted my AI to do the same.

That's what skills are. They're markdown files that define a process: "First explore the codebase, then propose approaches, then get my approval, then write tests, then implement." The AI follows the playbook instead of freestyling.

My Toolkit

Over the months, I tried a lot of skills. Some became daily habits. Others were interesting experiments that didn't stick. Here's what I actually use:

Skill What It Does

feature-devGuided feature development with codebase analysis
brainstormStructured ideation before touching code
playground-architectureInteractive architecture visualization
review-prMulti-dimensional PR code review
playground-brainstormInteractive brainstorm explorer
voiceVoice-to-text interaction for quick input
write-planDetailed step-by-step implementation plans
pr-adopterProcess and implement PR review feedback
execute-planExecute written plans with checkpoints
brainstorm-refineIterative idea refinement loops
browser-to-codeExtract context from browser tabs into workflows
agent-teamsMulti-agent parallel work
goal-writerPerformance goal drafting

If I had to pick just one, it's feature-dev. It's the thing I reach for most — when I know roughly what I want and need the AI to genuinely understand my codebase before touching anything.

But the interesting part isn't individual skills. It's how they chain together.

Three Workflows, Discovered by Accident

I didn't plan these. They emerged over weeks of trial and error.

The Full Pipeline

Early on, I was working on something architecturally complex — a new service with unclear boundaries. I found myself naturally falling into a three-step rhythm:

/superpowers:brainstorm  ->  /superpowers:write-plan  ->  /superpowers:execute-plan

First, I'd brainstorm with the AI. Not "generate ideas" brainstorming — more like a dialogue. It would ask me questions, I'd clarify my intent, it would propose two or three approaches, and we'd land on a spec document together. Then it would break that spec into tiny implementation steps — two to five minutes each, with exact file paths and code snippets. Then it would execute those steps one by one, spinning up fresh subagents for each task.

The whole thing felt like pair programming with someone who actually reads the brief.

I used this for anything greenfield. Building an auth system from scratch? Full pipeline. New API service? Full pipeline. The brainstorm step alone saved me from building the wrong thing more times than I can count, because it forced me to explain why before how.

But it's heavy. Three steps, lots of back-and-forth. After about a month, I realized most of my work didn't need that level of ceremony.

The Tight Loop

So I started skipping the middle step. Instead of brainstorm-plan-execute, I'd just brainstorm and then jump straight into feature-dev:

/superpowers:brainstorm  ->  /feature-dev  ->  (oh wait, new question)  ->  /superpowers:brainstorm  ->  /feature-dev

This works because feature-dev already does its own codebase analysis. And by this point, my CLAUDE.md files — the project instruction files that Claude reads at the start of every session — had gotten detailed enough that the AI already knew my conventions, my gotchas, my architecture. It didn't need a formal written plan anymore. The plan was in the CLAUDE.md.

This became my daily driver. Quick brainstorm to align on the approach, then straight into building. If something unexpected came up during implementation, I'd pop back into a brainstorm, think it through, and dive back in. Some afternoons I'd do five or six of these cycles, redesigning multiple components in one sitting.

The Review Pipeline

For codebases where I'm reviewing existing code rather than building new stuff, a different pattern emerged:

/pr-review-toolkit:review-pr  ->  evaluate findings  ->  pr-adopter  ->  implement changes

Review-PR is clever — it spins up specialized agents that look at security, performance, architecture, and testing in parallel. You get a multi-dimensional review in one shot. But the real gem is pr-adopter. It takes review comments and evaluates them before implementing. Is this comment actually valid? Is it high-impact or nitpicky? What's the risk of the suggested change?

That evaluation step is something I used to do manually, and honestly, I wasn't always great at it. Having the AI triage feedback before acting on it prevents a lot of wasted effort on low-value changes.

Superpowers: The Discipline Layer

The workflows above describe what I do. Superpowers is the plugin that enforces how I do it. It's an open-source collection of skills by Jesse Vincent that adds engineering discipline to Claude Code — and it's the single most important plugin I installed.

What makes Superpowers different from other skill packs is that it doesn't just add capabilities. It adds constraints. Three non-negotiable rules — "iron laws" — that the AI must follow no matter what. After ten weeks of using them, I can say with confidence: these constraints are what make AI-assisted development actually reliable.

Iron Law 1: No Production Code Without a Failing Test First

This is strict Test-Driven Development. The TDD skill enforces a red-green-refactor cycle:

  1. Red — Write a test that describes the behavior you want. Run it. Watch it fail.
  2. Green — Write the minimum code to make the test pass. Nothing more.
  3. Refactor — Clean up, remove duplication, improve names. Tests must stay green.

The rule is absolute: if you write production code before the test, delete it. Don't keep it as reference, don't adapt it. Delete and start fresh from the test.

This sounds extreme, but it solves a real problem with AI coding assistants. Without TDD, the AI tends to write large chunks of code that look correct but have subtle bugs. With TDD, every line of production code exists because a test demanded it. The test proves the code works — not a vibes check.

I'll admit I was skeptical at first. Making the AI delete code it just wrote felt wasteful. But after a few sessions, I noticed something: the code that came out of TDD cycles was consistently better — fewer bugs, cleaner interfaces, and easier to refactor later.

Iron Law 2: No Fixes Without Root Cause Investigation First

The systematic debugging skill enforces a four-phase process:

  1. Root Cause Investigation — Read error messages carefully (all of them, including stack traces). Reproduce the issue consistently. Understand why it's happening, not just what.
  2. Pattern Analysis — Is this a known pattern? Have similar issues occurred before? What's the broader context?
  3. Hypothesis & Testing — Form a specific hypothesis. Test it. If it's wrong, form another one. No shotgun debugging.
  4. Implementation — Only now do you fix it.

The key constraint: you cannot propose a fix until you've completed phase one. No guessing. No "let me just try this real quick." The AI must demonstrate it understands the root cause before touching any code.

This matters because AI assistants love to guess. They'll see an error, pattern-match to something similar they've seen, and suggest a fix that addresses the symptom but not the cause. The debugging skill prevents that by forcing investigation first. After three failed fix attempts, it goes further — it tells the AI to question the architecture, not just the code.

Iron Law 3: No Completion Claims Without Fresh Verification

The verification skill is deceptively simple: before claiming anything is done, you must run the verification command in the current message and show the output. Not "I ran the tests earlier." Not "the tests should pass." Run them now, show the results, prove it.

This catches a surprisingly common failure mode: the AI claiming tests pass based on stale information. Maybe it ran the tests three tool calls ago and things changed since. Maybe it's extrapolating from partial output. The verification skill eliminates that by demanding fresh evidence for every claim.

How They Work Together

The three iron laws form a cycle. You write a failing test (TDD), implement the fix, debug any issues (systematic debugging), and verify everything passes before calling it done (verification). Skip any step and the chain breaks.

In practice, Superpowers also includes the brainstorming, planning, and execution skills I described in the workflows section. But it's the iron laws that changed how I work. They turned "hope-driven development" — where you hope the AI got it right — into something I can actually trust.

The Skill That Surprised Me Most: Browser-to-Code

I built this one myself, and I almost didn't mention it because it sounds gimmicky. But it turned out to be genuinely useful, especially in enterprise settings.

The idea is simple: you have a bunch of browser tabs open — a ticket, a design, some API docs — and instead of copy-pasting content into your terminal, you let the AI read those tabs directly.

Under the hood, it uses chrome-cdp, a lightweight Chrome DevTools Protocol skill that connects to your browser via WebSocket. It can list your tabs, take screenshots, grab accessibility trees (structured representations of page content), and read HTML. All read-only — it never clicks or navigates in your browser.

The orchestrator skill, browser-to-code, wraps this into a workflow:

  1. It lists your open Chrome tabs
  2. You pick the relevant ones
  3. You categorize them — "this is the ticket," "this is the design," "this is reference docs"
  4. It extracts content from each (screenshots for visual stuff, structured text for everything else)
  5. It builds a summary and asks you to confirm
  6. You pick a workflow — brainstorm, plan, debug, or review
  7. It hands everything off to that skill with the full browser context included

Where This Really Shines: Enterprise Development

If you've ever worked with SAP systems or similar enterprise software, you know the pain. Your requirements are in ServiceNow. The current UI is in SAP Fiori. The API documentation is in some internal wiki. The BTP cockpit has your service bindings. That's four tabs of critical context that normally you'd have to mentally stitch together.

With browser-to-code, a typical session looks like this: I open my ServiceNow ticket showing the requirements, the Fiori app showing the current UI, and the OData API docs. I run /browser-to-code, select those three tabs, and the AI now has full visibility — the acceptance criteria from the ticket, a screenshot of the current Fiori screen, and the structured API surface. Then I pick "brainstorm" and we're off, designing the solution with all the context right there.

No copy-pasting. No "let me describe what the screen looks like." The AI literally sees it.

This is especially powerful for SAP Fiori or UI5 development where you might have the Business Application Studio open alongside Fiori Elements documentation and a BTP cockpit. Three complex web UIs that the AI can reason about together — something that would take paragraphs to explain in a prompt.

The Experiments

Not everything became a daily habit. Some skills were worth building even if they didn't stick.

Voice

For about a week, I went all-in on voice. I built two skills — listen-once for single commands, voice-conversation for continuous dialogue. The idea was to talk through ideas before typing them. And honestly, it worked. Speaking ideas aloud is more natural than writing them, especially when you're still fuzzy on what you want.

But the switching cost killed it. Going from voice to text to voice again felt clunky. I kept reaching for the keyboard mid-sentence to reference a file path or a function name. Voice is great for early-stage brainstorming, but the moment you need precision, text wins. I still use it occasionally, but it didn't become a habit.

Playgrounds: Architecture Explorer and Brainstorm Explorer

These are the skills I'm probably most proud of building. They generate interactive, self-contained HTML files — single-page applications you open in a browser to visually explore your codebase.

The Architecture Explorer creates a three-panel layout: a component tree on the left, workflow and data flow diagrams in the center, and a details panel with code snippets and annotations on the right. It supports four diagram views — workflow, data flow, file structure, and sequence diagrams — all generated from a real analysis of your codebase. Click on a component, see its file path, its responsibilities, and the actual interface it exposes. Right-click any element and the AI explains it, lists its dependencies, or suggests improvements — all in real-time.

The Brainstorm Explorer takes a different angle. It creates an interactive concept map where you can mark your knowledge level on each topic (know / fuzzy / unknown / explore), draw connections between concepts, and capture ideas that appear as nodes on the canvas. It's designed for the moment when you're dropped into an unfamiliar codebase and need to map what you know, what you don't, and where to dig first. It even generates prompts you can feed back to the AI for deeper exploration.

I used these a lot when onboarding to unfamiliar codebases — especially large monorepos where the architecture isn't obvious from the folder structure alone. The visual map they create is something I'd refer back to weeks later, long after the session that created them. If you work with complex projects, these are worth trying. I even wrote a small MCP bridge that connects the templates with Claude Code and makes them interactive.

Goal Writer

This one came out of a very specific frustration: writing performance goals for review cycles. Every year, you sit down, stare at a blank document, and try to turn vague aspirations into structured goals with success indicators. It's the kind of task that's not hard, exactly, but it's tedious and easy to do poorly.

So I built a skill for it. The goal-writer first asks about your organization's strategy, then your recent achievements, then your planned work for the coming period. It cross-references all of that and drafts goals that are actually aligned with what your org cares about — each with success indicators at three levels (partly achieved, achieved, exceeded). The structure forces specificity in a way that freeform writing doesn't.

I've only used it twice, but both times it turned a dreaded two-hour task into a thirty-minute conversation. It's the kind of niche skill that doesn't get daily use but absolutely earns its keep when the moment comes.

Agent Teams

The newest experiment, and the one I'm most excited about. Claude Code has a built-in concept of subagents — you can spawn child agents that work independently in the background while the main agent continues. Agent Teams take that a step further: you create a named team, define a shared task list, and spin up multiple agents that pick up tasks, work in parallel, and coordinate through messages.

 

Here's how it works in practice. You invoke /agent-teams:team-spawn with a preset — say "feature" for parallel feature development, or "review" for multi-dimensional code review. The team lead agent decomposes your task into subtasks with file ownership boundaries (so agents don't step on each other's changes), creates a task list, and spawns teammates. Each teammate claims a task, works on it, marks it done, and checks the list for more work. The lead monitors progress and synthesizes results when everything's done.

What makes this different from just running multiple prompts yourself is the coordination layer. Agents can message each other, block on dependencies ("don't start the API tests until the API is built"), and the shared task list gives everyone visibility into what's done and what's left. It's surprisingly close to how a real engineering team operates — just faster.

The coordination overhead is real, and I wouldn't use it for small tasks. But for something like "build this feature across three layers of the stack" or "review this PR from security, performance, and architecture angles simultaneously," it's a genuine force multiplier.

Claude Code Commands You Should Know

Before we move on, a quick detour. If you're using Claude Code and haven't explored its built-in commands, you're leaving a lot on the table. These aren't skills — they're native features of the CLI, and I use most of them daily.

/compact — This one saved me more times than any skill. When your conversation gets long and you start hitting context limits, /compact compresses prior messages while preserving the essential information. I used it constantly during deep implementation sessions. Don't wait until you get a context error — compact proactively.

/init — Generates a CLAUDE.md file for your project by analyzing your codebase. It's the starting point for teaching Claude about your conventions, architecture, and gotchas. I ran this once at the beginning and have been refining the file ever since. That file is arguably the single most impactful thing you can create for your AI workflow.

/review — Quick code review of your current changes. Less elaborate than the review-pr skill but perfect for a fast sanity check before committing.

/mcp — Lists your connected MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers. Essential if you're using skills that rely on external servers, like the playground sync server for real-time AI interaction in the browser.

/resume — Picks up where you left off. When you close a session and come back later, /resume restores the previous conversation so Claude remembers what you were working on. I use this constantly — real work rarely fits into a single session, and without resume you'd have to re-explain the entire context from scratch.

Plan mode — Not a slash command, but worth mentioning. When you type plan at the start of a prompt (or the AI enters plan mode automatically), Claude switches to read-only exploration. It can read files and search your codebase but won't edit anything until you approve its plan. I use this for anything non-trivial — it's the single biggest guard against the AI changing things you didn't want changed.

Shift+Tab — Toggles between different permission modes (plan mode, auto-accept, normal) without typing commands. A small convenience that adds up over hundreds of sessions.

Claude HUD — Install This First

If you take one thing from this section, make it this: install Claude HUD. It's a plugin by Jarrod Watts that adds a persistent status line below your input showing everything you need to know about your session at a glance.

[Opus | Pro] █████░░░░░ 45% | my-project git:(main*) | 2 CLAUDE.md | 5h: 25% | ⏱️ 5m
✓ Read ×3 | ✓ Edit ×1 | ⟳ Agent: Exploring auth patterns (12s)
✓ All todos complete (3/3)

That context bar alone is worth the install. Before HUD, I'd be deep in a session, spawn a few subagents, and suddenly hit the context limit with no warning. Now I can see the percentage fill up in real-time and /compact before it becomes a problem. The tool activity line shows exactly what Claude is doing — which files it's reading, which agents are running, how long they've been going. The todo progress tracks task completion when you're using structured workflows.

It sounds like a minor quality-of-life improvement. It's not. When you're running multi-agent sessions or deep implementation cycles, having real-time visibility into context usage, active agents, and task progress changes how you work. You make better decisions about when to compact, when to break a session, when to wait for a subagent versus starting something else.

Install it in three commands:

/plugin marketplace add jarrodwatts/claude-hud
/plugin install claude-hud
/claude-hud:setup

No restart needed. It just works.

How My Workflow Changed Week by Week

The first two weeks, I just used feature-dev for everything. It was good enough, and I didn't know what else was out there.

Week three, I discovered the brainstorm-plan-execute pipeline and it felt like a revelation. Finally, the AI was thinking before coding. I used the full pipeline religiously.

Week four, I got curious about voice and spent a week building and testing voice skills. Fun experiment, partial adoption.

Weeks five and six, I created the playground skills for architecture visualization. These became a go-to for understanding new codebases.

By weeks seven and eight, I'd streamlined. The full three-step pipeline was overkill for most tasks. I dropped write-plan and settled on brainstorm-to-feature-dev as my default loop.

Week nine, I started using git worktrees to keep feature branches isolated. Week ten, I tried agent teams for the first time.

The pattern: start formal, then streamline. The full pipeline taught me the value of each step. Once I'd internalized those patterns — always brainstorm first, always verify before calling something done — I could drop the ceremony without losing the discipline.

Why Some Skills Stick and Others Don't

After a few months, I can see the pattern clearly.

Skills that became habits solve a recurring pain point. Feature-dev, brainstorm, review-pr — I need these almost every session. They slot into my existing workflow instead of replacing it, and they have an obvious trigger. "I'm about to build something" means brainstorm. "There's a PR to review" means review-pr. No decision fatigue.

Skills I built but rarely use tend to be too specialized, or they overlap with things other tools already do well. Some required setup that broke my flow — by the time I'd configured everything, I'd lost the context I was trying to preserve.

But here's the thing: even the skills I don't use regularly were worth building. The process of defining a workflow — writing down the steps, thinking about what information flows where — clarifies your thinking. It's like writing documentation that you never read again. The act of writing it was the point.

What I'd Tell Someone Starting Out

Brainstorm before you build. Every time I skipped this step, I paid for it later. Five minutes of structured thinking saves thirty minutes of rework. It's the single highest-leverage habit I picked up.

Let your workflow evolve. Don't commit to a rigid process on day one. Start with the full pipeline, understand what each step gives you, then trim what you don't need. Your workflow in month two should look different from month one.

Invest in CLAUDE.md. This is the file that tells Claude about your project — conventions, architecture, gotchas. Every lesson I learned went into this file. Over time, it became comprehensive enough that the AI understood my codebase without a formal plan. It compounds in a way that's hard to appreciate until you've done it for a few weeks.

Build custom skills for things you repeat. Most skills are just 20-50 lines of markdown. That's twenty minutes of work to encode a process you'd otherwise re-explain every session. If you find yourself giving the same instructions more than twice, make it a skill.

If you work in enterprise environments, try browser-to-code. The copy-paste tax between ServiceNow, SAP, Confluence, and your terminal is real. Letting the AI see your browser tabs directly is a genuine quality-of-life improvement.

Manage your context window. Long sessions hit limits. I found myself compacting context constantly. It's better to break work into focused sessions than to fight the context ceiling.

What's Next

Multi-agent teams are where I'm spending my experimental energy now. The idea of decomposing a task into independent pieces and letting multiple agents work simultaneously — that feels like the next productivity jump. The coordination patterns aren't quite there yet, but the foundation is solid.

And browser-to-code has room to grow. Right now it reads individual tabs. Imagine if it could understand the relationships — this Jira epic links to those Figma designs which affect these API endpoints. Connected context rather than isolated snapshots.

But honestly, the biggest lesson from last months isn't about any specific skill. It's that the AI follows a process when you give it one, and freestyles when you don't. Skills turn freestyling into something structured and repeatable. The AI doesn't just write code — it thinks, plans, reviews, and iterates. And that process, unlike any individual prompt, gets better every time you refine it.

Links

  • Superpowers (core skills: brainstorm, write-plan, execute-plan, TDD, debugging, verification): github.com/obra/superpowers
  • Chrome CDP Skill (browser DevTools Protocol integration): github.com/pasky/chrome-cdp-skill
  • My Custom Skills (browser-to-code, goal-writer, architecture explorer, brainstorm explorer): github.tools.sap/Security-Hub/claude-skills
  • My Custom Skills for non SAP employees (architecture explorer, brainstorm explorer): https://github.com/Dimi82/claude-playground-explorer
  • Claude HUD (session status line — context usage, agent tracking, task progress): github.com/jarrodwatts/claude-hud
  • Claude Code Workflows (146 marketplace skills: feature-dev, agent-teams, PR review, and more): available via the Claude Code plugin marketplace

Claude Code is a powerful Agent and now officially available @SAP .

Hi @manu_xu,

Thank you for the detailed blog — it’s very helpful.

We are currently trying to implement this approach but are running into the following issue when executing the External API step from SAC Multi‑Actions:

The external API step “STARTAPITC” has failed due to:
The request response code 401 could not be recognized.

We are using a Technical User with OAuthClient, and the user is assigned a scoped role with the following authorizations:

Assigned Privileges:

  • Data Warehouse General (-R------) – To access SAP Datasphere
  • Data Warehouse Data Builder (CRUD----) – To create, edit, and delete task chains
  • Space Files (CRUD----) – To create, read, update, and delete objects in spaces

Additional privileges assigned (as documented for task chain execution and notifications):

  • Data Warehouse Data Integration (-R------) – View data integration task logs
  • Data Warehouse Data Integration (--U-----) – Manually run data integration tasks
  • Data Warehouse Data Integration (----E---) – Schedule data integration tasks
  • Data Warehouse Data Builder (------S-) – Share task chains to other spaces
  • User (R-------) – Display and add notification recipients when configuring email notifications

Despite these assignments, we consistently receive the 401 error during execution.

Could you please advise if:

  • Any additional privileges are required specifically for OAuth‑based execution via the External API step, or
  • There are known constraints or prerequisites  that might cause this behavior?

Any guidance or clarification would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks again for sharing this solution and for your support.

Best regards,
Srini

entry.title: SAP Cloud ERP Launchpad Extension v2.2: Fiori App Groups & Multi-Language Support Are Here
entry.content:
Hey SAP community,

It has already been more than a year since I released my leisure-time project: the SAP Cloud ERP Launchpad extension for Chrome and Edge — a small productivity tool designed to make daily work with Fiori apps and SAP Cloud ERP systems faster and more convenient.  Read more here.

 

Over the past year, I’ve received a lot of valuable feedback from users across the SAP community. Two feature requests came up again and again:

  • The ability to organize apps into folders

  • Language support for users who do not primarily work in English

I’m happy to announce that both features are now available with version 2.2.

 

 

📁Fiori App Groups (Folders)

You can now create Fiori App Groups directly on the Options page.

Right-click on the logo and select "Options".

Switch to the new table tab "Fiori App Group":

Create new Groups to structure your fiori apps in the popup list.

Switch to the Fiori App table tab and edit the fiori apps and assign them to your fiori app groups.

Enjoy a better structured fiori app list:

These groups work like folders where you can organize your Fiori apps in a way that matches your workflow. Simply assign apps to a group and structure your Launchpad the way you prefer.

You can also reorder the groups, allowing you to place the most frequently used ones at the top for quicker access.

This should make navigating large numbers of apps significantly easier.

 

 

🌍Language Support

Another highly requested feature was multi-language support.

You can now select your preferred language directly on the Options page, making the extension more accessible for SAP users around the world.

 

🙌Thanks for the Support

 

It’s been amazing to see how many people in the SAP community are using this extension in their daily work.

If you like the update, feel free to leave a like or a comment — feedback always helps shape the next features.

Thanks for supporting and using this tool!

 

Best,

Edrilan Berisha

Strategic Project Consultant to the Extended Board | Office of the GM & CPO - SAP Business Suite, Finance & Spend

entry.title: Re: Need to assign the Support role for KHC SAP support team
entry.content:
To allow the users provided to create support cases directly, it is necessary for one of the designated administrators—Super Administrators, Cloud Administrators, or User Administrators listed on the  Important Contacts page in SAP for Me to create an S-user ID for them. This S-user ID must also include the following case-related authorizations:   

  • Display Cases
  • Display all Cases
  • Report Technical Problem
  • Send Cases to SAP
  • Close Cases
  • Display Cloud Data (only needed for some Cloud Solutions KBA 2598170)

To move forward, could you please contact one of the administrators to create a new S-user for each of them and assign the required authorizations?

For detailed guidance on creating or managing S-user IDs, please refer to the following Knowledge Base Articles:  

1271482 - How does an administrator create or delete S-user IDs? - SAP for Me

1296527 - How to create a support case for SAP Support - SAP for Me

If you need assistance with the process of creating the S-user or assigning the necessary authorizations, please let me know, and I will be happy to provide further guidance or clarification. 

Best regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: P-User is not matching with ANID
entry.content:
Spoiler
We ran into a similar error when integrating contracts (4AZ using PurchaseContractReplicationRequest_In). The confirmation message (PurchaseContractStatusNotification_Out) was missing the Sender and Recipent Business System IDs, resulting in the error.

SAP provided a Note for this (3527847), which addressed the issue.
~~~~~~

3527847 - SOAP API PurchaseContractStatusNotification_Out: Sender and Recipient Business System IDs are missing in the message payload

Symptom

The contract sent from Ariba is created in S4 using eSOA message PurchaseContractReplicationRequest_In. However the PurchaseContractStatusNotification_Out message cannot be transfered back to Ariba due to CIG ErrorCode: CIG-PLT-00639.  The reason for the CIG error are the missing Sender and Recipent Business System IDs in the message payload.

The issue regarding the Sender System ID is only raised in case you do not use SLD for the maintenance of the Busines Systems in your system landascape. This note enables using the logical systems which are defined in transaction SCC4 (table T000).

Other Terms

PurchaseContractReplicationRequest_In, http://sap.com/xi/Procurement, CL_MMPUR_SOA_CONTR_IN, SOAP API, EDI Channel, LCR_GET_OWN_BUSINESS_SYSTEM, SPC_SCM1.

Reason and Prerequisites

Reason: Program Error.

Prerequisite: logical system has to defined in transaction SCC4 (table T000) correctly.

Solution

Apply the correction instructions attached with this note

entry.title: Mass Asset Transfer Across Company Codes in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

We are currently operating in an SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud environment and have recently created a new company code in the same country as an existing company code.

For asset migration between these company codes, we are currently using the Post Transfer – Across Company Codes Within the Same Country application. Using this app, we are able to transfer assets successfully; however, the process allows us to transfer only one asset at a time.

Since we have a large number of assets that need to be transferred to the new company code, performing the transfer individually for each asset is time-consuming.

We would like to understand:

  • Is there any standard functionality or application available in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud that supports mass transfer of assets across company codes within the same country?

  • Are there any best practices or recommended approaches for performing this activity efficiently in the Public Cloud environment?

Any guidance or suggestions would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance for your support.

Best regards,
Siva Nagi Reddy

entry.title: Mondayisation of Public Holidays
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

Are there any plans to enhance the Temporary Holiday Calendar functionality so that public holidays falling on a weekend are automatically moved to Monday (Mondayisation)?

We have a New Zealand client whose requirement is that if a public holiday falls on a weekend and employees are not scheduled to work, the holiday should be observed on Monday. However, if employees are expected to work on weekends, the holiday should remain on the actual weekend date.

While the Temporary Holiday Calendar can handle this scenario, maintaining it across multiple employee groups could become quite cumbersome. It would be helpful if this could be automated. Any thoughts or suggestions?

SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking 

SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking #temporaryassignment #timeoff #holidaycalendar

entry.title: 2-step wave Picking process needed for Public cloud EWM
entry.content:
Hi Community members,

If you are working or implementing SAP Public cloud with EWM, you may notice there is no 2-step wave picking in SAP Public cloud EWM. However SAP has pushed for default SAP public cloud selection for new customers, without this 2-step wave picking, large DC or warehouses with high product turn-over, high frequency of small picking will face a lot of challenges to improve the picking efficiency.

I have raised the improvement idea to add 2 step wave picking into public cloud, if you have the same needs, feel free to vote for it.

https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/idea/369407

Kind Regards

entry.title: Re: Resource Handover for Product WTs in S/4HANA Cloud Pu...
entry.content: Wow, Chris has covered almost every possible scenario. In Adam’s case, this is a fairly standard HU LOSC / POSC process. I’ve worked on a previous implementation where stock was transferred from one building to another, involving three to four handover stages. In this setup, the entire pallet—containing either a single product or multiple products—is packed into one HU. The process works as follows: the first operator picks one or more products into a pre-created, barcoded pick HU and drops it in the first hand‑shake area, completing the initial warehouse task. The second operator then scans the HU, and the system prompts them to move it to the next hand‑shake area, and so on, until the full process is completed. Hope this is much more easier understand description.

entry.title: Week 3 : Use RAP Business Logic Prediction to Implement Validation
entry.content:
Last week we had created a RAP Application and added some entries in it. We also explored how to use Joule's 'Explain' feature to understand a simple code added in the CDS Data Definition. 

This week let us go one step further and explore another very interesting feature of Joule's ABAP AI capability -  The RAP Business Logic Prediction.

Quick Links

Please use this separate thread to ask your questions and discuss issues. 

Sample Scenario

Here let us think about any simple scenario like changing a field to ‘Mandatory’ and then create a new entry without filling value for this field. Empty values should not be accepted for the mandatory field and we can use the ‘validation’ keyword to check if its kept empty. Here use Joule’s ‘RAP Predict Business Logic’ capability to suggest the code to raise an error message in the implementation method of the validation function. Let’s see the steps in detail.

Task1: Create a validation and implement the corresponding method.

  1. Create a validation in the BDEF of the CDS view. Open your behavior definition and specify one of the fields as mandatory.Define Mandatory Field
  2. Define the Validation in same BDEF of the CDS view.Validation Defenition
  3. In order to have draft instances being checked by validations before they become active, they have to be specified for the draft determine action prepare in the behavior definition. Replace the code line draft determine action Prepare; in your BDEF with the following code snippet as shown on the screenshot below.Prepare Action
  4. Save and activate the changes.
  5. Now you can notice a small icon to the left of the validation definition. Click on it to create the implementation method for adding validation logic in the local handler class of the behavior pool of your entity. Save and activate the changes. Hint: You can right click on the new method name and choose Quick Fix as well.Implement ValidationFor additional information on Validations and its syntax and use you can refer the BDEF ‘/DMO/R_Travel_D’. Alternatively you can also refer https://github.com/SAP-samples/abap-platform-rap100/tree/main/exercises/ex05

Task2: Enhance validation with RAP Predict Business Logic

Use Joule's RAP Predict Business Logic to add the logic to check whether the mandatory field created in Task 1 is filled while creating a new entry. If it is left empty throw an error message.

Note:- The logic and examples are mentioned just for your reference and to make the purpose of the challenge easy to understand. Please feel free to use your own logic and dataset.

To know how to use RAP Predict Business Logic follow this tutorial. Hint: You can right click on the new method name and choose Quick Fix as well.

Good luck and wait for the last challenge next week. Until then Stay Jouled !!!

entry.title: Building Enterprise-Grade RAG: Seven Architectural Innovations Beyond Vector Search
entry.content:
Building Enterprise-Grade RAG: Seven Architectural Innovations Beyond Vector Search

A concise technical guide for production RAG systems

Executive Summary

Traditional Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems rely primarily on semantic similarity through vector embeddings, but this approach fails in enterprise environments with large, diverse document collections. This article presents seven architectural innovations that address fundamental RAG limitations, delivering 60-75% faster queries, 85-95% precision, and 60-75% token reduction compared to traditional approaches.

The Five Problems with Traditional RAG

Problem 1: Semantic-Only Retrieval Cannot Distinguish Context

Traditional RAG encodes queries and searches all embeddings by cosine similarity, assuming semantic closeness equals relevance. This breaks down when users need contextual filtering: "show me vendor A contracts from 2024" retrieves any semantically similar contract language regardless of vendor identity, document type, or fiscal year. Vector embeddings capture meaning but not categorical properties—two vendor contracts from different years produce nearly identical embeddings because they use identical legal language and pricing terminology. Result: 30-50% false positives from wrong vendors, wrong years, or wrong document types. Users must manually verify every result, defeating automation's purpose.

Problem 2: Structural Blindness Destroys Document Organization

Character-count chunking (typically 1000 characters with 200 overlap) ignores document structure, splitting at arbitrary boundaries regardless of semantic sections. A document with clear sections-Introduction, Technical Specifications, Pricing, Compliance gets carved into chunk 1, chunk 2, chunk N without preserving these boundaries. When users query "show me technical specifications," the system retrieves random paragraphs mentioning "technical" scattered across the document rather than the complete specifications section. Even perfect vector search cannot overcome structural destruction during chunking, related content sits in disconnected chunks requiring manual reconstruction.

Problem 3: Table Destruction Loses Critical Structured Data

Tables contain 40-60% of critical enterprise information-pricing matrices, technical specifications, comparison charts, timelines, responsibility assignments. Text-based chunking catastrophically fails: a 20-row pricing table gets split across chunks, each missing column headers or context. Converting tables to linear text loses two-dimensional relationships. Worse, tables are typically introduced by explanatory paragraphs ("The following pricing applies to annual contracts only...") that get separated during chunking, leaving the LLM to interpret data without crucial qualifiers. Result: 60-80% of tables rendered unusable or misinterpreted.

Problem 4: Chat History Token Explosion from AI Response Storage

Most RAG systems store complete conversation history-user messages plus AI responses for follow-up context. A typical 5-turn conversation: user message 1 (50 tokens) + AI response 1 (2000 tokens) + user message 2 (30 tokens) + AI response 2 (1800 tokens) + user message 3 (40 tokens) + AI response 3 (2200 tokens) = 6,120 total tokens stored. Actual new information: 120 tokens from user messages. AI responses are synthesized from documents already in the database—storing them is pure redundancy adding no new context. As conversations extend to 10-15 turns, history consumes 20,000-30,000 tokens, leaving less context window for retrieved documents and increasing API costs exponentially.

Problem 5: One-Size-Fits-All Responses Waste Resources

Traditional RAG treats all queries identically: embed → vector search → retrieve → generate LLM response. But 30-40% of enterprise queries want document inventory (lists, counts, metadata): "list all contracts," "how many technical documents?" These need simple database lookups returning structured lists , not vector searches followed by verbose LLM generation. The remaining 60-70% need content extraction: "summarize technical requirements," "compare pricing across vendors." These require full RAG pipeline. Traditional systems waste compute and time generating responses for inventory queries that should be instant metadata lookups, degrading user experience with slow, verbose responses when users want clean, fast lists.

The Seven Innovations

Innovation 1: LLM-Powered Document Classification

Use an LLM to classify each chunk into semantic section types (Introduction, Technical Specifications, Requirements, Pricing, Timelines, Compliance, Risks) based on content analysis rather than arbitrary character boundaries. After standard text splitting creates chunks, pass each through a zero-temperature LLM with constrained classification prompt returning a single category label stored as chunk metadata. This adds some time consumed per chunk during ingestion but enables powerful section-aware retrieval and saves time during retrieval, users can query "show me pricing sections" and retrieve complete, focused pricing content rather than scattered paragraphs. Classification uses temperature=0 for deterministic results and minimal token output to reduce latency. Impact: Section-specific queries improve from 50-70% precision to 85-95%, with 60-80% reduction in irrelevant chunks.

Innovation 2: Dual-Stream Chunking (Text + Tables)

Implement parallel extraction pipelines-one for flowing text, one for structured data. During document processing, detect and extract tables, charts, and matrices as complete units separately from text extraction, preserving internal structure entirely. Convert tables to Markdown format for LLM readability, capture surrounding context (preceding 2-3 paragraphs explaining scope and assumptio, and infer semantic category from position. Store tables as independent chunks with specialized metadata: structure type, dimensions, contextual flags. Enrich table content with context prefix: "Context: The following pricing applies to annual contracts only. [Table: Pricing Matrix] | Plan | Cost | ..." This embeds business meaning with data structure, dramatically improving semantic search. Impact: 100% table structure preservation (vs 20-30% with text splitting), 90%+ precision for structured data queries, 50-70% better semantic relevance from context enrichment.

Innovation 3: Rich Metadata Layer

Attach comprehensive hierarchical metadata at three levels: folder/organizational (inherited from directory structure), document (file properties and custom business tags from document management systems), and chunk (section classification). Metadata captures identity (name, type, author), organizational context (department, region, category), temporal attributes (creation date, fiscal period), and custom business tags (vendor, client, project, status). Use inheritance pattern: folder-level tags apply to all contained documents, document-level tags override folder tags, chunk-level tags add section granularity. Store metadata as structured JSON alongside embeddings, enabling efficient database queries. On initialization, scan corpus to discover available metadata values across dimensions, building a catalog of unique vendors, document types, regions, years. Impact: Enables precise contextual queries combining 3-5 filters, reducing search space by 95-98% before vector operations.

Innovation 4: Metadata Pre-Filtering Before Vector Search (The Game Changer)

Execute metadata filtering BEFORE vector similarity search, reducing search space by to only contextually relevant candidates. Five-stage process:
(1) Discover available metadata by scanning corpus—vendors, regions, document types, fiscal years,
(2) LLM extracts relevant filters from user query given the discovered catalog, returning structured constraints like {vendor:[A,C], doc_type:[contract], year:[2024]}.
(3) Build database query matching extracted metadata conditions.
(4) Execute filter reducing 50,000 chunks to 500-1000 pre-qualified chunks (98%+ reduction).
(5) Compute vector similarity only on filtered subset. Modern vector databases (like HANA Cloud) support hybrid queries combining metadata filters with vector similarity. 
Impact: 3x faster, precision jumps from 15% to 95%+ because all results are contextually correct, 60-75% token reduction from tighter context.

Innovation 5: Smart Chat History (User Messages Only)

Store complete conversations in database for audit, but retrieve only user messages from time window (e.g., last 12 hours) when building context for new queries. AI responses are synthesized from documents already in database they add no new information, only consuming tokens. Use context retrieval of user messages only filtered by time window. Pass user-only history to LLM for query consolidation: "Given prior user queries [list], interpret new query in context." Time windowing captures typical work session duration while preventing stale context interference and naturally limiting context growth. Impact: 90-97% token reduction on chat history, faster query processing, better context quality focusing only on user intent refinement trail.

Innovation 6: Intent-Based Query Routing

Classify user intent before processing, routing to appropriate pipeline: lightweight metadata retrieval for inventory queries, full RAG for content extraction. Use fast keyword matching for unambiguous queries ("list documents," "how many," "find all" → LIST intent; "what are," "summarize," "compare," "extract" → ANSWERING intent). LIST intent executes database query returning document names/counts/categories as structured HTML list (no LLM). ANSWERING intent executes full enhanced RAG with metadata filter, vector search, and LLM generation. For ambiguous queries, use lightweight LLM classification or default to ANSWERING. Impact: 10x faster responses for inventory queries, 40-60% lower API costs, better UX matching response type to user expectation (clean lists for inventory, comprehensive synthesis for extraction).

Innovation 7: Production-Grade Resilience

Implement fault-tolerant batch processing for enterprise-scale ingestion (1000+ documents, 100,000+ chunks). Four-layer defense: (1) Batch processing with rate limiting-split into small batches (10-20 chunks per call) with delays between batches (1-2s) and documents (3-5s), (2) Independent document processing-each document is autonomous unit, failure doesn't abort batch, successful documents commit immediately, (3) Progress tracking—real-time logging, error categorization, partial success reporting ("150 succeeded, 8 failed"). (4)Before processing, check database for existing documents by source URL (prevents deduplications and saves time as everytime it runs it only uploads delta documents). 
Impact: Reliable operation at scale, survives transient API failures without intervention, clear progress visibility, partial success delivery (

Performance Impact: Traditional vs Enhanced

Metric

Traditional RAG

Enhanced RAG

Improvement

Query Latency

2-4 seconds

0.5-1.2 seconds

60-75% faster

Chunks Searched

10,000-100,000+ (all)

500-2,000 (1-5% of corpus)

95-98% reduction

Precision

50-70% relevant

85-95% relevant

30-40pp increase

False Positives

30-50% of results

5-15% of results

70-85% reduction

Token Usage (input)

12,000-20,000 tokens

3,000-8,000 tokens

60-75% reduction

Chat History Overhead

6,000-8,000 tokens

150-300 tokens

95-97% reduction

Inventory Query Time

2-4 seconds (full RAG)

0.1-0.3 seconds (metadata)

90-95% faster

Table Retrieval Success

20-40% intact

95-100% intact

3-4x improvement

Ingestion Success Rate

60-80% (fails on errors)

90-98% (fault tolerance)

15-30pp increase

The Core Architectural Insight:
 Metadata filtering before vector search beats pure vector search. By filtering 95-98% of irrelevant chunks using structured metadata before expensive semantic similarity calculations, we achieve simultaneous improvements in speed, accuracy, and efficiency—the rare engineering win-win-win.


Traditional RAG

The New Pipeline

Conclusion: 
These seven innovations transform RAG from prototype demo to production-grade system users can trust. The patterns are platform-agnostic and applicable to any enterprise RAG system over large, diverse document collections with organizational hierarchies, temporal dimensions, and categorical properties.

entry.title: Re: SAP B1 trying to load dimensions for Fixed Assets usi...
entry.content:
@David_Smith291 
In SAP Business One (SQL), assigning dimensions to Fixed Assets through DTW can sometimes be difficult because the standard template does not always expose the dimension fields clearly.

One option is to verify whether the Distribution Rule or Cost Center fields are available for mapping in the DTW template. Also ensure that the dimensions and distribution rules are already created and active before running the import.

In some projects, consultants handle this using the DTW update method or controlled SQL updates when mass changes are required.

I would also be interested to know if anyone has successfully mass loaded dimensions for Fixed Assets using DTW.

entry.title: Re: Mass Asset Transfer Across Company Codes in SAP S/4HA...
entry.content:
Good day @SivaNagiReddy 

Thank you for your questions.

Below are answers your specific questions:

Question: Is there any standard functionality or application available in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud that supports mass transfer of assets across company codes within the same country?

Answer:  Based on your description, the short answer would be YES, the Public Cloud offers a standard for mass transfer of assets.  The app you are currently using, is the recommended app for processing an individual intercompany transfer, so its one-by-one approach is aligned with its design. For large volumes, you have the option to use Upload Asset Details app, which includes Mass Transfer and separate guidance for Data Preparation for Mass Transfer of Assets.

Consult these references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/74ffc024fd794eeca49dc8ffe7f9870d/761a00e5c2294f6b87d7392c7d14687a.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/74ffc024fd794eeca49dc8ffe7f9870d/46a9aa881ff7417c86bae1ee9a700673.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/74ffc024fd794eeca49dc8ffe7f9870d/b688e1b067604fd69c27cd6545f81768.html?locale=en-US

Question: Are there any best practices or recommended approaches for performing this activity efficiently in the Public Cloud environment?

Answer: Yes, the approach SAP recommends is:

  • Use ABT1N/Post Transfer- Across Company Codes Within the Same Country only for low volume or exception-based single transfer.
  • Use Upload Asset Details for high-volume transfers.
  • Prepare and validate the transfer data in the Excel template before you upload.

See the following reference for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3e5fcf2c768746049b5627bd5a42f720/f0a16409fbc649b896add04c1c0a624e.html?locale=en-US

For your scenario with many assets, the best approach in Public Cloud is:

  1. Make use of Upload Asset Details instead of processing each asset manually in ABT1N.
  2. Ensure you download the mass transfer template.
  3. Populate the required source and target asset/company-code transfer fields.
  4. Ensure you carefully validate the dependencies for mass transfer preparation in the file.
  5. Ensure you only upload and execute the mass transfer in small batches if the required volume is high. The batching point is an implementation recommendation for control and reconciliation.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: How can we write back data from SAP Datasphere to S4H...
entry.content:
Please lookup replication flow documentation.

In option 1 (RF) - ABAP to process the data on the S4 side if you need it to be moved from one table to another with some upsert or delta logic. Given your replication flow pushes the data to the inbound/staging table on the S4 side.

In option 2 (OData) - Otherwise you just write ABAP which consumes OData from DSP in S4 and updates the target table/s 

Third option could in theory be using embedded BW in S4.
